reason: Assumed users wanted premium short-form content on mobile. Launched during COVID when everyone was at home with TVs. $1.75B burned in < 1 year.
Twitter acquired then neglected it. Lost creators to Instagram/Snapchat. No monetization for creators. Platform dependency on parent company.
Forced adoption through YouTube/Gmail. No unique value over Facebook. Users had no reason to maintain two social graphs.
Aggressive discounting attracted deal-seekers not loyal users. 25% repeat rate. Workers and customers bypassed the platform after first booking.
$699 juicer + DRM juice packs. Bloomberg proved you could squeeze packs by hand. Overengineered hardware for a problem nobody had.
Unit economics never worked: $150M raised, burning $7M/month. Inspections + delivery + guarantee = massive per-transaction cost.
Technology didn't work. Faked demos. Regulatory fraud. The product was physically impossible with current science.
Cyberbullying destroyed user experience. Growth via fear, not value. Raised $35M, shut down voluntarily.
Laundry has no frequency or margin to sustain VC-scale delivery logistics. Average order too small for unit economics.
Spotify outspent on licensing deals and user acquisition. No differentiation on catalog. Second-mover disadvantage in winner-take-all market.
Quality issues with UP band. Couldn't compete with Fitbit on price or Apple Watch on premium. Hardware margins too thin.
Twitter cut off social graph access. Then Twitter launched Periscope. Platform dependency + incumbent response.
Removed anonymity (the core feature) to fight bullying. Users left. Re-added it too late. Pivoted to identity-based and lost DNA.
Raised $41M pre-launch on proximity photo sharing concept. Nobody wanted to share photos with nearby strangers. Solution looking for a problem.
Flash sales model had no retention. Pivoted from gay social to design commerce to custom furniture. Each pivot burned cash. $300M raised.
Marketed as AI-powered but relied on manual bookkeepers behind the scenes. Couldn't scale the human labor. Misrepresented automation level.
Vertically integrated construction startup burned $2B. Factory-built modules didn't fit real-world sites. Regulatory complexity across states.
Blood test + coaching at $3,500/year. Insurance wouldn't cover it. Not enough people willing to pay out-of-pocket for prevention.
Andy Rubin couldn't compete with Apple/Samsung on distribution, ecosystem, or brand. $330M raised for a phone nobody asked for.
Sold $10/month unlimited movie tickets that cost $12+ each. Hoped theaters would subsidize; they didn't. Burned $40M/month.
Here One earbuds couldn't compete with AirPods. AR audio was too early. Hardware dev costs exceeded revenue. Apple killed the category.
Cooking + delivering fresh meals has terrible unit economics. Needed $15 average order but delivered $9 avg. Couldn't achieve density.
B2B body measurement tech. Market too niche. Acquired by Amazon for talent, product discontinued. Technology was ahead of market need.
Same issue as Homejoy: customers booked once for the discount, then hired the cleaner directly. Zero switching cost. No lock-in.
Ran out of cash overnight. Couldn't scale human assistants profitably. Revenue per assistant < cost per assistant.
Supreme Court ruled it violated copyright by retransmitting broadcast signals. Legal risk destroyed the business model entirely.
Offered pick-up + packaging + shipping for $5. Real cost was $10+. Unit economics never worked. Pivoted to SMB but too late.
$25M raised at Stanford. Product never shipped. Internal chaos, founder inexperience. Tried to compete with Venmo without a product.
Acquired by Lyft after failing to find unit economics. Car wash margin too low for on-demand logistics overhead.
Fingerprint payment system. Stores didn't want to install hardware. Consumers didn't trust biometrics. $340M raised, fraud charges.
